content:
# Genesis Chapter 40 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 23r–23v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_040_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_040_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 38–39 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0042

---

**1** And it came to pass after these things that the cupbearer of the king of Egypt and the baker sinned against their master the king of Egypt.

**2** And Pharaoh was angry with his two officers — the chief cupbearer and the chief baker.

**3** And he put them under guard in the house of the captain of the guard, into the house of confinement — the place where Joseph was confined.

**4** And the captain of the guard assigned Joseph to them, and he attended them, and they were in guard for some days.

**5** And the two of them dreamed a dream — both of them — each his own dream in one night, each according to the interpretation of his dream, the cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt who were confined in the house of confinement.

**6** And Joseph came to them in the morning and saw them, and behold, they were troubled.

**7** And he asked the officers of Pharaoh who were with him under guard in the house of his master, saying, "Why are your faces downcast today?"

**8** And they said to him, "We have dreamed a dream, and there is no one to interpret it." And Joseph said to them, "Do not interpretations belong to God? Tell it to me, please."

**9** And the chief cupbearer told his dream to Joseph and said to him, "In my dream, behold, a vine was before me.

**10** And in the vine were three branches, and it was as though it budded — its blossom came up, and its clusters ripened into grapes.

**11** And Pharaoh's cup was in my hand, and I took the grapes and pressed them into Pharaoh's cup, and I placed the cup upon Pharaoh's palm."

**12** And Joseph said to him, "This is its interpretation: the three branches are three days.

**13** Within yet three days Pharaoh will lift up your head and restore you to your place, and you will place Pharaoh's cup in his hand as was the former custom when you were his cupbearer.

**14** But remember me when it goes well for you, and please show me covenant-faithfulness — mention me to Pharaoh and bring me out of this house.

**15** For I was indeed stolen from the land of the Hebrews, and here also I have done nothing that they should put me into the pit."

**16** And the chief baker saw that he had interpreted well, and he said to Joseph, "I also — in my dream, behold, three wicker baskets were on my head.

**17** And in the uppermost basket were all kinds of food for Pharaoh — the work of a baker — and the birds were eating them from the basket on my head."

**18** And Joseph answered and said, "This is its interpretation: the three baskets are three days.

**19** Within yet three days Pharaoh will lift up your head from upon you and will hang you on a tree, and the birds will eat your flesh from upon you."

**20** And it came to pass on the third day — it was Pharaoh's birthday — and he made a feast for all his servants, and he lifted up the head of the chief cupbearer and the head of the chief baker in the midst of his servants.

**21** And he restored the chief cupbearer to his cupbearer's position, and he placed the cup upon Pharaoh's palm.

**22** And the chief baker he hanged — just as Joseph had interpreted to them.

**23** But the chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph, and he forgot him.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources.

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 40 spans Folios 23r–23v across two manuscript images.

   **Folio 23r** (GEN_040_page_001.jpg): This is PDF page 44 of the West Semitic Research Project sequence — the recto of Folio 23, photographed right-side up, with the folio number 23 clearly visible at the top. The page carries the conclusion of Genesis 39 in its opening lines (vv. 20–23 of chapter 39) before the parashah marker that introduces Genesis 40. The opening of Genesis 40 — וַיְהִי אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה חָטְאוּ מַשְׁקֵה מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם וְהָאֹפֶה ("and it came to pass after these things that the cupbearer of the king of Egypt and the baker sinned") — is clearly visible at the beginning of the new parashah section. The three-column layout with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks is legible throughout. The key terms מַשְׁקֵה (mashqeh — "cupbearer") and אֹפֶה (ofeh — "baker") are clearly visible with their characteristic vocalization. The central theological statement of verse 8 — הֲלוֹא לֵאלֹהִים פִּתְרֹנִים ("do not interpretations belong to God?") — is fully legible. The cupbearer's dream (vv. 9–11) and Joseph's interpretation with the plea (vv. 12–15) are clearly visible, including the key term בּוֹר (bor — "pit") in verse 15. The folio appears to carry the chapter through approximately verse 15, where the baker's dream begins.

   **Folio 23v** (GEN_040_page_002.jpg): The verso of Folio 23. This is PDF page 45. The image is scanned in an inverted orientation (bottom-to-top), but the Hebrew text is fully legible when reoriented. This page carries the baker's dream and Joseph's interpretation (vv. 16–19), the birthday feast of Pharaoh (v. 20), the restoration and the hanging (vv. 21–22), and the concluding sentence of the chapter — וְלֹא זָכַר שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים אֶת יוֹסֵף וַיִּשְׁכָּחֵהוּ ("but the chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph, and he forgot him," v. 23) — followed immediately by the beginning of Genesis 41. The key phrase וַיִּשְׁלַח פַּרְעֹה וַיִּקְרָא ("and Pharaoh sent and called," 41:1ff.) is visible beginning the new chapter. The central verb תָּלָה (talah — "to hang") in verse 22 is legible, and the phrase מֵעָלֶיךָ (me'aleykha — "from upon you") in verse 19 is visible, marking the devastating variation from verse 13.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Two Vaticanus pages were read directly.

   **Page 38** (GEN_040_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 38 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0042). This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 39 (vv. 19–23) and the beginning of Genesis 40. The chapter notation "CAP XLV" is visible in the right margin, marking the beginning of Genesis 40 in Vaticanus's chapter enumeration. The continuous uncial Greek text is legible in three columns. The Greek of Genesis 40 begins with the characteristic Vaticanus uncial hand: Εγένετο δε μετα τα ρήματα ταυτα ήμαρτον ό αρχιοινοχόος του βασιλέως Αιγύπτου ("and it came to pass after these things that the chief cupbearer of the king of Egypt sinned"). The key theological question of verse 8 — οὐχὶ διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ἡ διασάφησις αὐτῶν ἐστιν ("is not the interpretation of them through God?") — is identifiable in the text. This page carries the chapter through approximately verse 19, including the cupbearer's dream and Joseph's plea.

   **Page 39** (GEN_040_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 39 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0043). This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 40 (vv. 20–23) and the beginning of Genesis 41. The chapter notation "CAP XLVI" is visible in the right margin, marking the Vaticanus chapter boundary for Genesis 41. The Greek of the birthday feast (v. 20), the restoration of the cupbearer and hanging of the baker (vv. 21–22), and the concluding verse 23 — οὐκ ἐμνήσθη δε ό αρχιοινοχόος τοῦ Ιωσηφ, ἀλλ᾽ ἐπελάθετο αὐτοῦ ("but the chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph, but forgot him") — are legible. The Greek's use of ἀλλά ("but") to introduce the forgetting verb (ἐπιλανθάνομαι) rather than the simple waw-conjunction of the MT is clearly visible.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "Sinned against their master" — חָטְאוּ לַאֲדֹנֵיהֶם (chate'u la'adonehem) (v. 1)**

The chapter's opening verb חָטְאוּ (chate'u — Qal perfect third masculine plural of חָטָא, chata' — "to sin, to miss the mark, to commit an offense") is the identical root Joseph used in 39:9 when he refused Potiphar's wife: "how could I sin against God?" (וְחָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים — ve-chatati l'Elohim). The narrator's choice to open chapter 40 with the same root is not coincidental. Chapter 39 ends with Joseph imprisoned for a sin he did not commit — he refused to sin (chata') against God. Chapter 40 opens with two officials who did sin (chate'u) against their master. The word links the innocent man who goes to prison because he refused to sin with the guilty men who go to prison because they did sin. Joseph will be the one through whom their imprisonment is interpreted and resolved.

The preposition לְ (le — "against, to") with the verb chata' constructs the sin as a relational offense directed at a person — "sinned against their master" (chate'u la'adonehem). This is the same construction as chatati l'Elohim in 39:9: "sinned against God." In both cases, the sin is directional — not merely an act in the abstract but an act against a specific relationship and authority. The narrative frames the cupbearer and baker as men who have offended their master (Pharaoh) — the nature of their offense is not specified; rabbinic tradition suggests a fly found in the wine or a pebble found in the bread, but the text does not say. The LXX renders ἥμαρτον τῷ κυρίῳ αὐτῶν ("they sinned against their lord/master") — consistent with the MT.

The formal translation renders "sinned against their master" precisely — both the verb (chata' → "sinned") and the relational direction (la'adonehem → "against their master"). This preserves the key verbal echo with 39:9 that marks Joseph as the innocent man whose righteousness put him in the prison where the guilty men are now confined.

**2. "The chief cupbearer and the chief baker" — שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים וְשַׂר הָאֹפִים (sar ha-mashqim ve-sar ha-ofim) (vv. 2, 9, 16, 20–22)**

The two officials are designated throughout the chapter by the compound title שַׂר X — "chief/officer/commander of the X." The word שַׂר (sar) denotes a high official, a commander, a chief officer — the same title used for the captain of the guard (שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים — sar ha-tabachim) in chapter 39. Their specific functions:

- מַשְׁקֶה (mashqeh) — "cupbearer, one who gives drink": from the root שָׁקָה (shaqah — "to water, to give drink"). The mashqeh was a high court official responsible for the king's wine service — not merely a servant but a trusted courtier with direct access to the royal person. The role carried political significance precisely because the cupbearer handled the king's cup, a position of intimacy and trust.

- אֹפֶה (ofeh) — "baker, one who bakes": from the root אָפָה (afah — "to bake"). Similarly, the royal baker was a trusted court official responsible for the preparation of the king's food. The two roles together represent the provision of food and drink — the most intimate domestic service.

The titles שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים and שַׂר הָאֹפִים — "chief of the cupbearers" and "chief of the bakers" — are plural construct forms: these men are the chiefs over the entire corps of cupbearers and bakers. They are not merely servants but officials of high rank who happen to have been guilty of some offense against Pharaoh. Their social status makes their imprisonment significant — and their release equally significant.

The LXX renders both titles with the prefix ἀρχι- (archi- — "chief"): ἀρχιοινοχόος ("chief wine-pourer/cupbearer") and ἀρχισιτοποιός ("chief bread-maker/baker"). The LXX maintains the sar/chief distinction with the same Greek prefix throughout. The formal translation renders "chief cupbearer" and "chief baker" — preserving sar as "chief" and translating the function words into English equivalents.

**3. "Do not interpretations belong to God?" — הֲלוֹא לֵאלֹהִים פִּתְרֹנִים (halo l'Elohim pitronim) (v. 8)**

Joseph's response to the officials' troubled faces is one of the most theologically dense sentences in the chapter: הֲלוֹא לֵאלֹהִים פִּתְרֹנִים סַפְּרוּ נָא לִי ("do not interpretations belong to God? Tell them to me, please"). Several elements demand attention.

The rhetorical question הֲלוֹא לֵאלֹהִים פִּתְרֹנִים (halo l'Elohim pitronim) is a halo-question — a negative question expecting an affirmative answer: "Is it not the case that interpretations belong to God? — Yes, they do." The particle הֲלֹא (halo — "is it not?") introduces a presumed-true assertion. The word order places the prepositional phrase לֵאלֹהִים (l'Elohim — "to God, belonging to God") emphatically before the subject: "to God — interpretations!" The emphasis falls on the divine ownership of the interpretive capacity, not on the speaker.

The key noun פִּתְרֹן (pitron — "interpretation, solution") is rare in the Hebrew Bible, appearing only in Genesis 40–41, where it occurs nine times. It derives from a root פָּתַר (patar — "to interpret, to unravel, to unfasten") that is also Semitic — compare Akkadian paṭāru, "to release, to loosen, to interpret." The pitron is the "loosening" of what is bound up in the symbolic dream — the making explicit of what is implicit. Joseph's claim is that this capacity for interpretation belongs to God (l'Elohim): God is the source, the interpreter, the one who holds the meaning. Joseph is the instrument, not the origin.

This claim is the chapter's theological center. Joseph does not say "I am an interpreter of dreams" — he says "interpretations belong to God." Then, in the very next breath: "tell them to me, please." The juxtaposition is deliberate: Joseph acknowledges that the interpretive gift comes from God and immediately offers to be its human conduit. This is neither false modesty nor self-promotion; it is a precise theological positioning — Joseph as the Spirit-gifted mediator of divine knowledge, not its autonomous possessor. The same pattern appears in 41:16, when Pharaoh asks whether Joseph can interpret, and Joseph replies: "It is not in me — God will answer Pharaoh's peace." Divine source, human mediation.

The use of Elohim (rather than YHWH) is consistent with the pattern of chapter 39: in speech to non-Israelite characters, Joseph uses the universally comprehensible divine name Elohim. The narrator uses YHWH throughout the Joseph narrative (39:2–5, 21, 23) when speaking of the divine presence, but Joseph uses Elohim when speaking to Egyptians. The formal translation renders "God" for Elohim here — the appropriate rendering of the common divine name.

The LXX renders this phrase οὐχὶ διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ἡ διασάφησις αὐτῶν ἐστιν ("is not the interpretation of them through God?") — using διά ("through") rather than the simple dative of possession used in the MT's prepositional phrase. The LXX's διά τοῦ θεοῦ ("through God") slightly shifts the emphasis from divine ownership ("interpretations belong to God") to divine agency ("interpretations come through God") — but the theological substance is equivalent. The MT's locution is more direct and more emphatic about divine possession of this capacity.

**4. "Lift up your head" — יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת רֹאשְׁךָ (yissa Far'oh et roshekha) (vv. 13, 19)**

The chapter's most devastating literary device is the double deployment of a single phrase. In verse 13, Joseph interprets the cupbearer's dream: עוֹד שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת רֹאשֶׁךָ וַהֲשִׁיבְךָ עַל נַכּוֹנֶךָ ("within yet three days Pharaoh will lift up your head and restore you to your place"). In verse 19, Joseph interprets the baker's dream with a variation: עוֹד שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת רֹאשְׁךָ מֵעָלֶיךָ וְתָלָה אוֹתְךָ עַל עֵץ ("within yet three days Pharaoh will lift up your head from upon you and will hang you on a tree").

The phrase נָשָׂא אֶת רֹאשׁ (nasa' et rosh — "to lift up the head") has a double semantic range in Biblical Hebrew:

1. **Idiomatic positive meaning**: "to favor, to restore to honor, to count" — as in a census (Numbers 1:2: שְׂאוּ אֶת רֹאשׁ — "lift up the head [of the people]," i.e., take a census), or in the idiom of lifting one's face/head as a sign of restored honor and acceptance. In verse 13, the phrase means "restore to your position" — Pharaoh will raise the cupbearer's standing, reinstate him to his role.

2. **Literal physical meaning**: when followed by the devastating qualification מֵעָלֶיךָ (me'aleykha — "from upon you"), the idiom shifts to its literal register — Pharaoh will physically lift the baker's head off his body. Decapitation followed by public display of the body on a tree.

The Hebrew text deploys exactly the same phrase in both verses — יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת רֹאשְׁךָ — and differentiates the fates only by the appended preposition מֵעָלֶיךָ ("from upon you") in verse 19. The baker hears verse 13 and is encouraged — he sees the cupbearer's favorable interpretation and believes his own will be similar (v. 16: "and the chief baker saw that he had interpreted well"). The formal parallelism of the two interpretations is engineered to allow the baker to misread his own fate until the additional words land with full force.

The formal translation preserves the identical phrase "Pharaoh will lift up your head" in both verses and then adds "from upon you" in verse 19 — allowing the English reader to experience the same double movement the Hebrew creates. Translations that render verse 13 as "restore you to your position" and verse 19 as "will have your head cut off" (NIV) destroy the verbal parallelism that is the chapter's central literary and theological device. The formal rendering preserves the shared phrase and marks the divergence only through the added qualifier, as the Hebrew does.

The LXX renders verse 13 as ἀρεῖ Φαραω τὴν κεφαλήν σου ("Pharaoh will take up your head") and verse 19 as ἀφελεῖ Φαραω τὴν κεφαλήν σου ἀπό σου ("Pharaoh will remove your head from you") — using two different Greek verbs (αἴρω in v. 13, ἀφαιρέω in v. 19) rather than the single Hebrew verb nasa' in both. The LXX thus differentiates the two verses lexically, partially dissolving the deliberate Hebrew ambiguity. The MT's single verbal root is formally significant and is preserved in the formal translation.

**5. "I was indeed stolen" — גֻּנֹּב גֻּנַּבְתִּי (gunnov gunnabti) (v. 15)**

Joseph's plea to the cupbearer in verses 14–15 contains one of the most personally revealing statements in the Joseph narrative. The phrase גֻּנֹּב גֻּנַּבְתִּי מֵאֶרֶץ הָעִבְרִים (gunnov gunnabti me'erets ha-ivrim — "I was indeed stolen from the land of the Hebrews") deploys the infinitive absolute construction: the infinitive גֻּנֹּב (gunnov — Pual infinitive absolute of גָּנַב, ganav — "to steal") is placed before the finite verb גֻּנַּבְתִּי (gunnabti — Pual perfect first person singular: "I was stolen") to create intensive emphasis — "I was stolen, truly stolen" / "I was indeed stolen." The Pual form throughout indicates passive voice with an implicit agent: "I was made to be stolen," i.e., "someone stole me."

The use of ganav ("to steal") rather than makar ("to sell") or laqach ("to take") is Joseph's own characterization of what happened to him. The text of Genesis 37:28 says the brothers "sold" (vayyimkeru — vayyimkar) Joseph to the Ishmaelites. Joseph says he was "stolen." From Joseph's perspective, there is no meaningful distinction — whether sold by brothers or stolen by traffickers, he was taken against his will, removed from his rightful place, treated as property. The verb ganav covers both: theft is the taking of something that does not belong to the taker.

The same infinitive absolute construction occurs elsewhere in the Joseph narrative for self-referential emphasis: in 44:28, Jacob says about his son "he was indeed torn to pieces" (tarof toraf — also an infinitive absolute), using the idiom Joseph's brothers used to deceive Jacob in 37:33. The Joseph narrative is dense with these echoing emphases.

The phrase "from the land of the Hebrews" (me'erets ha-ivrim) is notable: Joseph identifies his origin not as "Canaan" (the standard geographical designation) but as "the land of the Hebrews" — using the ethnic self-designation that the narrative has also placed in Potiphar's wife's accusation ("a Hebrew man," 39:14). Joseph adopts the ethnic name here as his own identity marker, both self-identifying and appealing to the foreignness and injustice of his situation. He was taken from his own people's land.

The formal translation renders "I was indeed stolen" — preserving the intensifying infinitive absolute as "indeed" — and "from the land of the Hebrews" precisely.

**6. "The pit" — הַבּוֹר (ha-bor) (v. 15)**

At the end of verse 15, Joseph says: וְגַם פֹּה לֹא עָשִׂיתִי מְאוּמָה כִּי שָׂמוּ אֹתִי בַּבּוֹר ("and here also I have done nothing that they should put me into the pit"). The word בּוֹר (bor — "pit, cistern, dungeon") is here the same word used in Genesis 37:22 and 37:24 when Reuben counseled the brothers not to kill Joseph but to throw him into the בּוֹר — the empty pit in the wilderness. The word appears in chapter 37 four times (37:20, 22, 24, 28) to describe the pit into which Joseph was cast before being sold.

Now, in chapter 40:15, Joseph uses bor for the prison — the same word, applied to his current confinement. The lexical choice is deliberate and carries profound narrative weight. For Joseph, the pit in the wilderness (bor) and the prison in Egypt (also bor) are the same thing: places of unjust confinement, pits of descent into which he has been thrown by others' choices. The word bor thus creates a subterranean verbal thread running through the Joseph narrative — each bor is another iteration of his casting-down, each bor is the darkness before the LORD lifts him up.

The LXX renders bor in verse 15 as λάκκος ("pit, cistern, tank") — the same word used in Genesis 37:22–24 for the pit of the wilderness. The LXX's consistency confirms the lexical link is recognized in the ancient reading tradition. The formal translation renders "pit" throughout — both in Genesis 37 (where it refers to the wilderness pit) and here in Genesis 40 (where it refers to the prison) — preserving the verbal echo that the Hebrew creates.

The use of bor for prison/dungeon is attested in other biblical texts: Psalm 28:1 ("those who go down to the pit"), Isaiah 38:18 ("the pit"), Zechariah 9:11 ("the waterless pit" as a place of captivity). The connotation of bor is always downward, dark, and confined — linguistically appropriate for both a literal pit and a prison cell.

**7. "Wicker baskets" — סַלֵּי חֹרִי (sallei chori) (v. 16)**

The baker's dream features three סַלֵּי חֹרִי (sallei chori — "wicker/white baskets") on his head, with the food for Pharaoh in the uppermost basket. The phrase סַל חֹרִי (sal chori) is debated. The word סַל (sal) denotes a basket — the same word used in verse 17 and throughout. The word חֹרִי (chori) is more uncertain. Options include:

1. **"White baskets"** — from חוּר (chur — "white, linen"), thus "white linen baskets" or "wicker baskets made of white material."
2. **"Wicker baskets"** — understood from context as baskets made of woven material; some suggest connection to a root meaning "perforated, with holes" — appropriate for a bread-basket with ventilation.
3. **"Of bread/grain"** — some read chori as related to the word for bread or grain in a variant sense.

The LXX renders τρεῖς κανοῦ χονδριτῶν ("three baskets of coarse-grain bread") — apparently reading chori as related to food content rather than basket material. This reading diverges significantly from the MT. The MT's phrase "wicker baskets of chori" most naturally refers to a type or material of basket rather than its contents (the contents are specified in v. 17). The formal translation renders "wicker baskets" — the most widely attested interpretation of chori as a basket type — while noting the LXX variant.

**8. "On the third day — it was Pharaoh's birthday" — בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי יוֹם הֻלֶּדֶת אֶת פַּרְעֹה (ba-yom ha-shelishi yom huledet et Par'oh) (v. 20)**

The phrase יוֹם הֻלֶּדֶת (yom huledet — "birthday, day of birth") is unique in the Hebrew Bible — this is the only occurrence of this phrase in the entire Old Testament. The word הֻלֶּדֶת (huledet) is the Hophal infinitive construct of יָלַד (yalad — "to bear, to give birth") — literally "the day of being-born-of Pharaoh," the day Pharaoh was born. The phrase "birthday" is therefore an implied celebration — a day when kings characteristically showed favor (by releasing prisoners, granting pardons, making feasts) as well as executing those who had offended them. The double fate of the two officials — one restored, one executed — on Pharaoh's birthday follows this ancient Near Eastern pattern of royal birthday clemency/judgment.

The only other "birthday" feast in the Old Testament occurs in Job 1:4–5 (where Job's sons "made a feast on their day"), but the term is different. The term יוֹם הֻלֶּדֶת occurs elsewhere in the New Testament: Matthew 14:6 and Mark 6:21, where Herod's birthday feast results in the execution of John the Baptist — the New Testament echoing the Genesis pattern of a royal birthday leading to an unjust death, with a mediating figure (Herodias / the servant Joseph) and a restoration/denial dynamic.

**9. "He forgot him" — וַיִּשְׁכָּחֵהוּ (vayyishkachehu) (v. 23)**

The chapter closes with a doubled statement of forgetting: וְלֹא זָכַר שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים אֶת יוֹסֵף וַיִּשְׁכָּחֵהוּ ("but the chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph, and he forgot him"). The two verbs express the same reality from opposite angles: לֹא זָכַר (lo zakar — "did not remember") uses the negation of the positive verb for memory; וַיִּשְׁכָּחֵהוּ (vayyishkachehu — "he forgot him") uses the positive verb for forgetting. The redundancy is deliberate and emotionally weighted: the narrator insists on the completeness of the forgetting — not merely a failure to remember but an active forgetting of Joseph as a person.

The verb זָכַר (zakar — "to remember") is one of the Old Testament's theologically significant verbs — God "remembers" Noah (8:1: וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת נֹחַ — "and God remembered Noah"), God "remembers" Rachel (30:22: וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת רָחֵל), God "remembers" the covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Exodus 2:24). Human forgetting (vv. 23) in the Joseph narrative stands in ironic counterpoint to divine remembering — the cupbearer forgets Joseph, but the LORD (who has been described as "with Joseph" throughout chapters 39–40) does not forget. The forgetting of v. 23 extends Joseph's suffering by two full years (41:1) but does not alter the LORD's providential trajectory.

The formal translation renders both verbs: "did not remember Joseph, and he forgot him" — preserving both the negated memory verb and the positive forgetting verb, with the conjunction "and" (waw) linking them. Many modern translations collapse this to "he forgot Joseph" (NIV) or a similar simplification, losing the deliberate verbal doubling. The formal translation preserves the doublet.

The LXX renders: οὐκ ἐμνήσθη δε ό αρχιοινοχόος τοῦ Ιωσηφ, ἀλλ᾽ ἐπελάθετο αὐτοῦ ("but the chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph, but forgot him"). The LXX uses ἀλλά ("but, however") rather than the simple conjunction to introduce the forgetting verb — a slightly stronger contrastive sense than the MT's waw. Both Greek verbs — ἐμνήσθη (aorist passive of μιμνήσκω — "to remember") and ἐπελάθετο (aorist middle of ἐπιλανθάνομαι — "to forget, to neglect") — are standard renderings. The MT is followed for its more understated, waw-linked doublet, which carries its own weight through simplicity.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 40

Pages 38–39 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The continuous uncial Greek text was legible throughout both pages, with the characteristic Vaticanus hand visible. Page 38 (pa_0042) carries the conclusion of Genesis 39 and the opening of Genesis 40 through approximately verse 19. The chapter notation "CAP XLV" is visible in the right margin of page 38. Page 39 (pa_0043) carries Genesis 40:20–23 and the beginning of Genesis 41, with "CAP XLVI" visible in the right margin for the Genesis 41 chapter notation. Key variants and equivalences observed:

| Verse (MT) | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 40:1 | חָטְאוּ מַשְׁקֵה מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם וְהָאֹפֶה ("the cupbearer of the king of Egypt and the baker sinned") | ἥμαρτον ὁ ἀρχιοινοχόος τοῦ βασιλέως Αἰγύπτου ("the chief cupbearer of the king of Egypt sinned") | MT followed | LXX adds ἀρχι- (chief) prefix to the cupbearer title in v. 1, anticipating the fuller title שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים (sar ha-mashqim) that appears in vv. 2, 9, 20–22. MT's opening is more concise: "the cupbearer ... and the baker." |
| 40:3 | בֵּית הַסֹּהַר ("house of confinement") | τὸ δεσμωτήριον ("the prison") | "house of confinement" (MT) | LXX consistently renders beit ha-sohar as δεσμωτήριον ("prison"); the formal translation preserves the Hebrew compound; see note on GEN_039 (identical decision). |
| 40:4 | יְמִים ("some days") | ἱκανάς ("sufficient [days], some time") | "some days" | MT's יָמִים (yamim — "days") here is indefinite ("days" = an unspecified period). LXX adds ἱκανάς ("sufficient, considerable") suggesting "quite some days" — implying a longer period. The MT's indefiniteness is preserved. |
| 40:5 | וַיַּחַלְמוּ חֲלוֹם שְׁנֵיהֶם אִישׁ חֲלֹמוֹ ("and the two of them dreamed a dream — each his own dream") | ἑκάτερος κατὰ τὸ ἑαυτοῦ ἐνύπνιον ("each according to his own dream") | "both of them — each his own dream" (MT) | MT emphasizes both the duality (shenehem — "the two of them") and the individuality (ish chalomo — "each his own dream"). LXX renders ἑκάτερος ("each of two") — which covers both aspects. MT's doublet structure is more emphatic. |
| 40:8 | הֲלוֹא לֵאלֹהִים פִּתְרֹנִים ("do not interpretations belong to God?") | οὐχὶ διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ἡ διασάφησις αὐτῶν ἐστιν ("is not the interpretation of them through God?") | MT followed | LXX renders: (1) pitronim (plural "interpretations") as ἡ διασάφησις (singular "the interpretation"); (2) l'Elohim ("to/belonging to God") as διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ("through God") — dative of agent vs. possessive. MT's plural emphasizes the general principle; LXX's singular narrows to the specific situation. MT's possessive locus of interpretation with God is theologically more striking than LXX's agentive "through." |
| 40:10 | וְהִוא כְּפֹרַחַת עָלְתָה נִצָּהּ ("and it was as though it budded — its blossom came up") | ἡ βλαστήσασα... ἀνήνεγκεν βότρυς σταφυλῆς ("having sprouted... bore clusters of grapes") | MT followed | MT's sequence of verbs — "budded/blossomed" (porachat), "came up" (altah nitzah), "ripened" (hivu) — gives a vivid progressive sequence. LXX compresses somewhat. MT's progression of the vine's stages is preserved. |
| 40:13 | עַל נַכּוֹנֶךָ ("to your place / upon your position") | ἐπί τὴν ἀρχήν σου ("to your office/rule") | "to your place" | MT's nakhon/nakhonekha is the term for "the established/fixed place" — one's proper position or rank. LXX renders ἀρχή ("office, rule, beginning") — appropriate for an official's role. The formal translation uses "place" for MT's more spatially grounded term. |
| 40:15 | גֻּנֹּב גֻּנַּבְתִּי ("I was indeed stolen") | κλοπῇ ἐκλάπην ("I was stolen by stealing") | "I was indeed stolen" | Both MT and LXX use the cognate intensifying construction (infinitive absolute in MT; dative-of-manner cognate noun in LXX). LXX's κλοπῇ ἐκλάπην literally "I was stolen by/with theft" — the cognate internal accusative achieves the same emphasis as the Hebrew infinitive absolute. Renderings equivalent; MT followed. |
| 40:16 | סַלֵּי חֹרִי ("wicker/white baskets") | τρεῖς κανοῦ χονδριτῶν ("three baskets of coarse-grain bread") | "wicker baskets" (MT) | The LXX reads chori as the food content (reading it as related to groats/coarse grain) rather than the basket type. This diverges significantly from the MT. Most scholars follow the MT's reading of chori as a basket material/type. |
| 40:19 | יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת רֹאשְׁךָ מֵעָלֶיךָ ("Pharaoh will lift up your head from upon you") | ἀφελεῖ Φαραω τὴν κεφαλήν σου ἀπό σου ("Pharaoh will remove your head from you") | MT followed | LXX uses ἀφαιρέω ("to remove, take away") in v. 19 vs. αἴρω ("to lift, take up") in v. 13 — two distinct verbs, dissolving the MT's deliberate single-verb parallel. MT's use of the identical verb nasa' (נָשָׂא) in both vv. 13 and 19 is the chapter's central literary device; formal translation preserves it. |
| 40:20 | יוֹם הֻלֶּדֶת אֶת פַּרְעֹה ("Pharaoh's birthday") | γενέσια Φαραω ("Pharaoh's birthday feast / Pharaoh's anniversary") | "Pharaoh's birthday" | LXX uses γενέσια (genesia) — a word that in classical Greek designated the anniversary commemoration of the dead (a "genesis-day" of the deceased), though in Hellenistic usage it came to mean "birthday celebration." The MT's yom huledet is unambiguously a birth-day (day of being-born); LXX's genesia is consistent with that meaning in its Hellenistic sense. |
| 40:22 | כַּאֲשֶׁר פָּתַר לָהֶם יוֹסֵף ("just as Joseph had interpreted to them") | καθὰ συνέκρινεν αὐτοῖς Ιωσηφ ("just as Joseph had decided/judged for them") | MT followed | LXX renders the verb פָּתַר (patar — "to interpret") with συγκρίνω ("to judge, to decide, to combine") — a more judicial rendering than the interpretive "interpret." MT's patar is the chapter's key word for dream-interpretation; it is rendered "interpret" throughout. |
| 40:23 | וְלֹא זָכַר... וַיִּשְׁכָּחֵהוּ ("and he did not remember... and he forgot him") | οὐκ ἐμνήσθη... ἀλλ᾽ ἐπελάθετο ("did not remember... but forgot") | MT followed | LXX uses ἀλλά ("but") for the second clause rather than the MT's simple waw conjunction. MT's understated doublet with a plain conjunction is more tonally flat and therefore more poignant; the LXX's contrastive ἀλλά is more rhetorical. MT's subdued repetition is preserved. |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Forgetting and Remembering**

Genesis 40 is framed by two memorable statements at opposite ends of the chapter. It opens with the words חָטְאוּ ("they sinned") connecting the imprisoned officials to the moral world of chapter 39, and it closes with וַיִּשְׁכָּחֵהוּ ("he forgot him") — the word that suspends Joseph in continued imprisonment for two more years. The narrative engine of Genesis 40 is therefore forgetting: human forgetting as the mechanism of delayed divine providence.

The chapter's closure with forgetting is the more striking when set against the pattern of divine remembering that structures the wider narrative. God "remembered" Noah (8:1), God "remembered" Rachel (30:22), God will "remember" his covenant with Abraham (Exodus 2:24). In Genesis 40, the human instrument of Joseph's rescue forgets — but the narrative reader knows that the LORD who has been consistently said to be "with Joseph" (39:2, 3, 21, 23) does not forget. The cupbearer's forgetting is real and causes real suffering (41:1 specifies two full years). But it is not the final word. The final word in the book is not "he forgot" — it is 50:24–25, where Joseph himself, at the moment of his death, invokes the divine remembering: "God will surely remember you" (פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד אֶתְכֶם אֱלֹהִים — paqod yifqod etkhem Elohim). The verb is paqad ("to visit, to remember with action") rather than zakar ("to remember") — but both words inhabit the same semantic domain of memory-with-consequence. The forgetting of the cupbearer in 40:23 is the last act before the chain of events that will lead Joseph from prison to palace — initiated precisely by the failure of the cupbearer's memory, and then resolved when the cupbearer finally does remember (41:9: "and the chief cupbearer spoke to Pharaoh, saying, 'I remember my offenses today'").

**The Dream as Medium of Providence**

Genesis 40 introduces the dream as the primary medium of Joseph's interpretive gift — and therefore as the primary medium of divine communication in the Joseph narrative's second half. The chapter establishes the interpretive pattern that will repeat in Genesis 41: (1) an important person dreams a significant dream; (2) no one can interpret it; (3) Joseph is brought in; (4) Joseph attributes the interpretive capacity to God; (5) Joseph interprets; (6) the interpretation comes true.

The theological framework of verse 8 — "do not interpretations belong to God?" — governs all subsequent dream-interpretation in the narrative. Dreams in the ancient Near East were widely understood as communications from the divine realm, but their interpretation required specialists (Egyptian dream-interpreters, Babylonian diviners). Joseph's claim is that the ultimate interpreter is God himself — the human interpreter is a transparent conduit. This positions Joseph not as a professional dream-interpreter (the role would be fully secular in an Egyptian context) but as a man through whom the God of Israel speaks into a foreign court. The theology of Joseph's gift is that it is precisely that — a gift from God, not a technique.

The two dreams of chapter 40 also establish the structural principle of the chapter 41 pair: two dreams with the same meaning (two officials, three days each; Pharaoh's two dreams, seven years twice). The doubling of dreams is itself a divine confirmation — in 41:32 Joseph will explicitly articulate this: "and the doubling of Pharaoh's dream means the thing is established by God and God will shortly bring it about." The pattern is introduced here with the two officials, each with three-day dreams, before it is deployed with cosmic significance in chapter 41.

**The Pit and the Prison: Joseph's Dual Bor**

Joseph's use of the word בּוֹר (bor — "pit") in verse 15 to refer to the Egyptian prison is the chapter's most charged verbal echo. When Joseph says "they put me into the pit" (שָׂמוּ אֹתִי בַּבּוֹר — samu oti ba-bor), he is simultaneously describing the prison where he now stands speaking to the cupbearer and invoking the image of the wilderness pit into which his brothers threw him in Genesis 37. The word bor bridges these two locations — both are places of unjust confinement, both involve others acting against him, both precede a providential "lifting up."

The trajectory of Joseph in the Joseph narrative can be mapped through the word יָרַד/bor:

- **Genesis 37:24–25**: Cast into the bor (pit) by his brothers — "and the pit was empty, no water in it" — then drawn up (vayyaaluhuvayya'alu — "they drew him up") and sold.
- **Genesis 39:20**: Imprisoned in the beit ha-sohar (house of confinement), but the LORD is with him.
- **Genesis 40:15**: The prison is a bor — "they put me into the pit."
- **Genesis 41:14**: "And Pharaoh sent and called for Joseph, and they brought him quickly out of the pit" (בּוֹר — bor) — the same word for his release from the Egyptian prison.

The bor language thus frames the entirety of Joseph's Egyptian captivity with pit imagery: he is in the pit from the moment the brothers throw him in (37:24) until Pharaoh summons him out (41:14). Every stage of his imprisonment is a bor — a confinement into which he has been cast by others' wrongdoing, and from which he will eventually be drawn up by divine providence. The pit is never final; Joseph always emerges from it. But each bor costs him — months, years, the prime of his young life.

**The Guilty, the Innocent, and the Interpreter**

The chapter presents a morally precise triangulation: two guilty men (who sinned against Pharaoh), one innocent man (who has done nothing — v. 15: לֹא עָשִׂיתִי מְאוּמָה). The guilty men are in prison; the innocent man is in prison. The guilty men will be sorted — one restored, one executed — on the basis of Pharaoh's sovereign judgment. The innocent man remains in prison after both guilty men have been resolved. The moral asymmetry is stark and pointed: guilt and innocence are not the decisive factors in whether one is imprisoned or released. What is decisive is the LORD's timing and the human instrument of remembering/forgetting.

Joseph's statement in verse 15 — "I have done nothing that they should put me into the pit" — is the chapter's most direct assertion of his innocence. The narrator has already shown the reader that this is true (39:7–12); now Joseph himself states it to another character for the first time. He does not complain to God; he makes his case to a human who has power to intervene. His appeal — "remember me... mention me to Pharaoh" (vv. 14–15) — is not theologically naive (he has just declared that interpretations belong to God); it is the proper use of human means in reliance on divine providence. Joseph does what he can; the outcome is in the LORD's hands.

The cupbearer's forgetting in verse 23 is thus not merely a plot mechanism — it is a theological statement about the limits of human instrumentality. The cupbearer who was going to be Joseph's advocate proves to be unreliable. The LORD who has been with Joseph all along proves to be the only reliable agent. Two years of silence and continued imprisonment (41:1) are the cost of the cupbearer's memory failure — but they also place Joseph's release precisely in the moment when Pharaoh will need an interpreter for his own dreams. The "forgotten" Joseph, retrieved from the bor at exactly the right moment, is the one whose providential trajectory has been set not by human remembering but by divine timing.

---

— LOGOS
